#1 Posterior Predctive Checks on Base Bayesian Model - Don River

Dylan

2026-04-03

Load Observed Data and Bayesian Models

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.2.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(here)
## here() starts at /Users/dylanthackeray/Documents/GitHub/toronto_flood_cat_model
library(evd)
library(posterior)
## This is posterior version 1.6.1
## 
## Attaching package: 'posterior'
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     mad, sd, var
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     %in%, match
set.seed(1234)

# Load observed data
severity_df <- read_csv(here::here("data", "model", "severity_data.csv"))
## Rows: 54 Columns: 1
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl (1): exceedance
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
freq_df     <- read_csv(here::here("data", "model", "frequency_data.csv"))
## Rows: 34 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl (2): year, n_events
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
exceedances <- severity_df$exceedance

# Load Bayesian fits
bayesian_pois <- readRDS(here::here("data", "model", "bayesian_model_fits", "poisson_fit.rds"))
bayesian_gpd  <- readRDS(here::here("data", "model", "bayesian_model_fits", "gpd_fit.rds"))

Extract Posterior Draws

# Extract summaries
pois_draws <- as_draws_df(bayesian_pois$draws())
gpd_draws  <- as_draws_df(bayesian_gpd$draws())

# Combine relevant parameters
posterior_df <- tibble(
  lambda = pois_draws$lambda,
  sigma  = gpd_draws$sigma,
  xi     = gpd_draws$xi
)

# Sample subset for speed
posterior_sample <- posterior_df %>%
  sample_n(500)

Trace Plots (HMC diagnostics)

library(bayesplot)
## This is bayesplot version 1.15.0
## - Online documentation and vignettes at mc-stan.org/bayesplot
## - bayesplot theme set to bayesplot::theme_default()
##    * Does _not_ affect other ggplot2 plots
##    * See ?bayesplot_theme_set for details on theme setting
## 
## Attaching package: 'bayesplot'
## The following object is masked from 'package:posterior':
## 
##     rhat
# Extract draws in correct format
pois_draws_array <- bayesian_pois$draws()
gpd_draws_array <- bayesian_gpd$draws()

# Combine parameters into one object
combined_draws <- cbind(
  as_draws_df(pois_draws_array)[, c("lambda", ".chain", ".iteration")],
  as_draws_df(gpd_draws_array)[, c("sigma", "xi")]
)
## Warning: Dropping 'draws_df' class as required metadata was removed.
## Warning: Dropping 'draws_df' class as required metadata was removed.
# Convert to proper draws object
combined_draws <- as_draws_array(combined_draws)

# Plot traces
# Poisson trace
mcmc_trace(
  bayesian_pois$draws(),
  pars = c("lambda")
)

# GPD trace
mcmc_trace(
  bayesian_gpd$draws(),
  pars = c("sigma", "xi")
)

# Prior * Likehood -> Posterior Plot
{pois_draws_array <- as_draws_df(pois_draws_array)
gpd_draws_array <- as_draws_df(gpd_draws_array)

    # Grids for each parameter
lambda_vals <- seq(0.5, 3, length.out = 200)
sigma_vals  <- seq(5, 25, length.out = 200)
xi_vals     <- seq(-0.5, 0.5, length.out = 200)

    # Fix other Parameters (Posterior means)
lambda_fix <- mean(pois_draws$lambda)
sigma_fix  <- mean(gpd_draws$sigma)
xi_fix     <- mean(gpd_draws$xi)

# Poisson ~ λ
lik_lambda <- sapply(lambda_vals, function(l) {
  exp(sum(dpois(freq_df$n_events, l, log = TRUE)))
})
lik_lambda <- lik_lambda / max(lik_lambda)

# GPD ~ σ
lik_sigma <- sapply(sigma_vals, function(s) {
  exp(sum(dgpd(exceedances,
               loc = 0,
               scale = s,
               shape = xi_fix,
               log = TRUE)))
})
lik_sigma <- lik_sigma / max(lik_sigma)

# GPD ~ ξ
lik_xi <- sapply(xi_vals, function(x) {
  exp(sum(dgpd(exceedances,
               loc = 0,
               scale = sigma_fix,
               shape = x,
               log = TRUE)))
})
lik_xi <- lik_xi / max(lik_xi)

prior_lambda <- dgamma(lambda_vals, shape = 2, rate = 1)
prior_sigma  <- dgamma(sigma_vals, shape = 2, rate = 0.1)
prior_xi     <- dnorm(xi_vals, mean = -0.3, sd = 0.3)

post_lambda <- density(pois_draws$lambda)
post_sigma  <- density(gpd_draws$sigma)
post_xi     <- density(gpd_draws$xi)

df_lambda <- tibble(
  x = lambda_vals,
  Prior = prior_lambda,
  Likelihood = lik_lambda,
  Parameter = "lambda"
)

df_sigma <- tibble(
  x = sigma_vals,
  Prior = prior_sigma,
  Likelihood = lik_sigma,
  Parameter = "sigma"
)

df_xi <- tibble(
  x = xi_vals,
  Prior = prior_xi,
  Likelihood = lik_xi,
  Parameter = "xi"
)

df_all <- bind_rows(df_lambda, df_sigma, df_xi) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Prior, Likelihood),
               names_to = "Type",
               values_to = "Density")


post_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(x = post_lambda$x, Density = post_lambda$y, Parameter = "lambda"),
  tibble(x = post_sigma$x, Density = post_sigma$y, Parameter = "sigma"),
  tibble(x = post_xi$x, Density = post_xi$y, Parameter = "xi")
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = df_all,
            aes(x = x, y = Density, color = Type),
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  geom_line(data = post_df,
            aes(x = x, y = Density),
            color = "black",
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  facet_wrap(~Parameter, scales = "free") +
  
  labs(
    title = "Prior vs Likelihood vs Posterior (All Parameters)",
    subtitle = "Black = Posterior (Stan), Colored = Prior & Likelihood",
    x = "Parameter Value",
    y = "Density"
  )
}

The trace plots confirm that all parameters have converged and are well-sampled. However, the shape parameter ξ shows higher posterior variance, indicating that while the inference is computationally stable, tail behavior remains weakly identified due to limited extreme observations.

Interpretation of Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior

The prior, likelihood, and posterior distributions illustrate how information is updated within the Bayesian framework.

The priors are intentionally weak and diffuse, reflecting limited prior knowledge and allowing the data to drive inference. As a result, the likelihood plays a dominant role in shaping the posterior distribution.

For parameters such as λ (event frequency) and σ (scale), the likelihood is relatively concentrated, indicating that these parameters are well-identified by the data. Consequently, the posterior distributions closely align with the likelihood, with only minor influence from the prior.

In contrast, the shape parameter ξ exhibits substantially greater uncertainty. The likelihood for ξ is relatively flat due to the limited number of extreme observations, meaning the data provides weak information about tail behavior. As a result, the posterior remains diffuse and is more sensitive to prior assumptions.

This highlights a key challenge in extreme value modeling: while central behavior can be estimated reliably, tail behavior is inherently data-sparse and difficult to identify. The Bayesian framework makes this uncertainty explicit, rather than masking it through point estimates.

Simulate From Posterior

sim_counts <- c()
sim_exceedances <- list()

for(i in 1:nrow(posterior_sample)) {
  
  lambda <- posterior_sample$lambda[i]
  sigma  <- posterior_sample$sigma[i]
  xi     <- posterior_sample$xi[i]
  
  # Simulate yearly event count
  N <- rpois(1, lambda)
  sim_counts <- c(sim_counts, N) #scale for density rather than the frequency. 
  
  # Simulate exceedances
  if(N > 0) {
    X <- rgpd(N, loc = 0, scale = sigma, shape = xi)
    sim_exceedances[[i]] <- X
  }
}

sim_exceedances <- unlist(sim_exceedances)

Frequency Plot (λ)

ggplot() +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = freq_df$n_events, y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5, bins = 10) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = sim_counts, y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "red", alpha = 0.5, bins = 10) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs Simulated Event Counts",
    subtitle = "red: Simulated Posterior Values | blue: Observed Values",
    x = "Number of Events per Year",
    y = "Frequency"
  )

Severity Plot Parameters: {σ, ξ}

ggplot() +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = exceedances, y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 20, alpha = 0.5,
                 fill = "blue") +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = sim_exceedances, y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 20, alpha = 0.5,
                 fill = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs Simulated Exceedances",
    subtitle = "red: Simulated Posterior Values | blue: Observed Values",
    x = "Exceedance (Q_peak - u)",
    y = "Frequency"
  )

Tail Behaviour via Survival Function

# Empirical survival
empirical_df <- tibble(x = exceedances) %>%
  arrange(x) %>%
  mutate(survival = 1 - (row_number()/n()))

# Simulated survival
sim_df <- tibble(x = sim_exceedances) %>%
  arrange(x) %>%
  mutate(survival = 1 - (row_number()/n()))

ggplot() +
  geom_step(data = empirical_df,
            aes(x = x, y = survival),
            linewidth = 1) +
  geom_step(data = sim_df,
            aes(x = x, y = survival),
            linetype = "dashed",
            linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "Tail Comparison (Log)",
    subtitle = "dashed: simulated survival function | solid: observed survival function",
    x = "Exceedance",
    y = "Survival Probability"
  )
## Warning in scale_y_log10(): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
## log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.

The survival function comparison shows strong agreement between observed and simulated data in the central region, indicating that the scale parameter (σ) is well-identified and accurately captures the typical magnitude of exceedances.

However, divergence in the upper tail reflects sensitivity to the shape parameter (ξ), which governs the rate of tail decay. The variability observed in the simulated tail arises from posterior uncertainty in ξ, rather than systematic bias, indicating that extreme behavior is weakly identified by the available data.

Numerical Summary Statistics

# Observed stats
obs_stats <- tibble(
  mean = mean(exceedances),
  p95  = quantile(exceedances, 0.95),
  max  = max(exceedances)
)

# Simulated stats (repeat many times)
sim_stats <- tibble(
  mean = mean(sim_exceedances),
  p95  = quantile(sim_exceedances, 0.95),
  max  = max(sim_exceedances)
)

obs_stats
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##    mean   p95   max
##   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
## 1  13.4  42.2    68
sim_stats
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##    mean   p95   max
##   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
## 1  14.7  45.5  201.

The large difference in maximum values reflects the sensitivity of extreme outcomes to the shape parameter (ξ), rather than a failure of the model to reproduce observed data.

Summary

The posterior predictive simulations reproduce the central behavior and upper quantiles of the observed data well, but generate larger extreme values. This divergence is driven by uncertainty in the shape parameter (ξ), which controls tail behavior.

Even small positive values of ξ imply a heavy-tailed distribution, meaning the model assigns non-negligible probability to extreme events beyond those observed historically. As a result, the model does not simply replicate past data, but represents a range of plausible future outcomes under the inferred process.

This highlights a fundamental limitation of extreme value modeling: tail behavior is weakly identified due to data sparsity. The Bayesian framework makes this uncertainty explicit, allowing it to propagate into downstream risk estimates such as AEP curves.
